main_sections.title: Neem Baba
main_sections.prose_summary: Amber, a schoolgirl, returns home and sits under the neem tree in her courtyard. She imagines the tree is whispering to her and begins a dialogue with it, addressing it as 'Neem Baba'. The conversation is structured in two numbered parts. In Part I, Neem Baba reveals its ancient origins — born millions of years ago in North India or Myanmar — and the spread of its family across Asian, African, and American countries. It shares the story of its names: Sanskrit names like Arishta (the one who cures diseases), Nimba, and Nimbaca, the name 'Neem' given by Iranians, the Hindi name Nimb, and regional Indian names including Nim, Leemba, Nimori, Nimbamond, Nimbe, and Nimo. Scientists have given it epithets such as 'bitter grace of God', 'nature's gift to man', 'cleanliness-parting tree', 'magic tree', and 'the tree of the twentieth century'. In Part II, Amber raises practical uses she knows — cleaning teeth with twigs, protecting clothes with leaves, and using neem leaves for measles. Neem Baba explains the chemical properties of neem leaves that destroy germs and relieve itching, and lists the medicinal uses of its leaves, bark, flowers, fruits, and roots. The dialogue continues into agricultural benefits — neem seed powder deters locusts, stops mosquito breeding in rice fields, increases crop yield, and protects against termites. Neem also preserves stored grains, is used in soaps and toothpaste, protects furniture from termites, and purifies the air. The exchange is warm and educational, with Amber demonstrating curiosity and existing knowledge, and Neem Baba offering gentle wisdom and an invitation for future discovery. The story is adapted from Neem Baba by S. I. Farooqi.

listening.transcript_text:
Rahul: Maa, have you heard about Peepal Baba? Our teacher told us about him today.
Mother: I have not heard about him. Who is he and what does he do?
Rahul: He plants trees along with his team. He also started a movement to plant and save trees.
Mother: That's wonderful. Where did he get this idea from?
Rahul: When he was studying in a school in Pune, his English teacher gave him the idea to plant trees. He was just 11 years old.
Mother: How did he plant so many trees all over the country as a child?
Rahul: He was born to an army officer's family in Chandigarh and whenever they were moved to a new city, he planted more trees.
Mother: But how did he come to be known as Peepal Baba?
Rahul: He used to plant Peepal and Neem trees as they have many uses for us and told others to do so. So, in the beginning, people made fun of him by calling him Peepal Baba but he was not bothered and continued to do his work. Now everyone calls him by that name lovingly.
Mother: He is really doing great work. We should learn from him and plant more trees.
Rahul: Yes, Maa. We must tell others to do the same.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will now listen to a boy speaking to his mother. As you listen, select the correct statements.

question_bank.stem: The mother was surprised to know that Peepal Baba had planted trees all over the country.
The mother disliked the work being done by Peepal Baba.
The mother wanted to know more about Peepal Baba.
The boy decided to take care of trees by listening to Peepal Baba's journey.
The boy was worried about what his teacher had told him.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Collect dry leaves of four different trees and plants around you. (1) Make one card: on the top, draw the shape of the leaf looking at the dry leaf and colour it. Inside the card, write the name of the tree/plant the leaf is taken from and one of its uses. (2) Now, speak about the whole process of making the card in front of the class. (3) Ask your classmates to identify the leaf and tell them one of its uses. After they answer, open the card, and share what you have written.

You have read about Neem Baba and learnt many things about the neem tree. Look at the trees in your neighbourhood and write a paragraph about any one tree that you like. Include: where it is (roadside/park, etc.), how it looks (height, tree trunk, leaves, flowers, etc.), why you like it (shade, medicinal properties, shelter, beauty, etc.), and give a title to your paragraph.

question_bank.stem: Write a paragraph about a tree in your neighbourhood. Cover: where it is, how it looks, why you like it. Give the paragraph a title.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study these words from the text: something, neem oil, cleanliness-parting, well known, grandmother, seed powder, childhood, well trained, thought-provoking. Put the compound words given above in their trees. These words are known as compound words. There are three types: Open compound words (spaces between the words), Closed compound words (no spaces between the words), Hyphenated compound words (hyphens between the words).

question_bank.stem: Sort the compound words into their correct tree: Open compound words | Closed compound words | Hyphenated compound words. Words: something, neem oil, cleanliness-parting, well known, grandmother, seed powder, childhood, well trained, thought-provoking.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Words that tell us what a person is doing are called verbs. Match the verbs in the first column showing action in the present to verbs in the second column showing action in the past. Make a sentence with any one form of the verb.

question_bank.stem: Match present-tense verbs to their past-tense forms. Present: become, find, make, discover, tell, give | Past: (i) made, (ii) gave, (iii) told, (iv) became, (v) found, (vi) discovered. Make a sentence with any one form.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Amber speaks to Neem Baba as if speaking to a person. This figure of speech is called personification. Look at some more examples: 'The leaves whispered in the wind.' 'The flowers danced merrily in the breeze.' Now, discuss it with your classmates and teacher.

When people from Persia came to India and saw that a common tree of their country was freely growing here, they started calling the tree Azad Darakhte Hindi. So, the scientific name of Neem in Latin became Azadirachta indica. Neem has different names in the Indian languages. Discuss with your teacher and find out which Indian languages these names belong to: Nimb, Nim, Leemba, Nimori, Nimbamond, Nimbe, Nimo.

question_bank.stem: Find out which Indian languages each neem name belongs to and fill in the table: Name | Nimb | Nim | Leemba | Nimori | Nimbamond | Nimbe | Nimo | Indian language

main_sections.title: What a Bird Thought
main_sections.poem_text:
I lived first in a little house,
And lived there very well,
I thought the world was small and round,
And made of pale, blue shell.

I lived next in a little nest,
Nor needed any other,
I thought the world was made of straw,
And nestled by my mother.

One day, I fluttered from my nest,
To see what I could find,
I said the world is made of leaves,
I have been very blind.

At length I flew beyond the tree,
Quite fit for grown-up labours,
I don't know how the world is made,
And neither do my neighbours.

Anonymous
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: The poem traces a baby bird's growing understanding of its world across four stanzas, each representing a stage of life. In the egg (little house), the world is small, round, and pale blue — literally the shell itself. In the nest, the world is warm straw and the mother's presence. When the fledgling first ventures out, it sees leaves and realises with humility that it had 'been very blind'. Finally, flying beyond the tree as an adult, the bird discovers that the world is immense and unknowable — even its neighbours share this wonder. The poem's central theme is the expansion of perception with experience, and the wisdom that grows from acknowledging what one does not know. The tone moves from confidence through self-correction to philosophical acceptance. The dominant device is metaphor — the bird's stages map onto childhood, adolescence, and adulthood — with rhyme (well/shell, other/mother, find/blind, labours/neighbours) giving a gentle, song-like quality.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

(3) The bird becomes blind due to leaves. (4) The bird flies away as an adult. (5) The bird is unhappy with the straw nest. (6) The bird lived in a cosy nest with its mother.

listening.transcript_text:
I'm a parrot, my name is Paro,
My feathers are bright and they glow.
In the morning, I spread my wings,
Fly around and see wonderful things.
Come with me through the skies so blue,
To places old and places new.
Among the trees, where the breeze is light,
Many new stories, oh, what a sight!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a short poem. As you listen, fill in the missing words. You can listen to the poem more than once.

I have…], where do you live [I live in… It is (where)…], what do you do during the day [I… I also…], do you have any message for humans [I want to tell the humans…].

Read a few more describing words: pointed, green, thin, brown, small, wooden, hanging, round. Now, use the describing words given in the box to create six sentences based on the given picture.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The bird's first home was made of a shell. Later its home was among leaves and made of straw. (I) Look at your home carefully and make a list of all the materials it is made of. Why do you think our homes are not made of straw? If you had to own a home of your choice, what would it look like and what materials would you like to use? How is a home different from a house? Discuss with your classmates and teacher.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: (II) Look at the different kinds of nests and homes the birds make. Have you seen any? Discuss these type of nests with your teacher and classmates. Look at the list of some workers who build houses. Find out the work they do and write, as shown in the example. You can think of other workers and add them too.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table: Workers and their work. Workers given: painter, electrician, plumber, carpenter, architect (add two more of your own). Example: mason — lays bricks and stones.

main_sections.title: Spices that Heal Us
main_sections.prose_summary: This section opens with two pre-reading activities: students recall three spices used at home and match pictures of ten spices (turmeric, fenugreek, cumin seeds, asafoetida, cinnamon, clove, ginger, black pepper, fennel seeds, cardamom) to their names in their own language and in English. The main text is a letter from Daadi (grandmother) to her grandchildren Vikram and Vaibhavi, who have recovered from illness using natural cures she had earlier shared. Daadi traces her knowledge to her own grandmother and proceeds to describe the healing benefits of common kitchen spices: turmeric (haldi) improves energy, digestion, and reduces body pain; fenugreek (methi) seeds soaked overnight manage blood sugar and body weight; cumin (jeera) aids digestion and cures sleeplessness; asafoetida (heeng) relieves gas and controls cough and cold; cinnamon (dalchini) and clove (laung) give relief from toothache; ginger (adrak) helps with cough, cold, and pain, and has been used in cooking for over 4000 years; black pepper (kali mirch) aids digestion and relieves body pain; fennel seeds (saunf) and carom seeds (ajwain) aid digestion after meals; cardamom (elaichi) helps with digestion, respiratory troubles, and bad breath. Daadi closes by advising the children to consult an elder before using any natural cure and to share these remedies with friends. The text emphasises intergenerational transmission of traditional knowledge. A listening task introduces Tulsi (Holy Basil) — found in north central India, called 'the Queen of Herbs', which decreases glucose and cholesterol levels, treats breathing and digestion disorders, has anti-bacterial properties used in hand sanitisers, and forms the base for mouth wash and toothpaste; Tulsi tea calms the mind and the plant protects from pollutants.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

listening.transcript_text: The wonder herb: Tulsi is a shrub in the basil family. It is said that it was first found in north central India and now grows almost everywhere in India. It is known as 'the Queen of Herbs' and has many medicinal uses. Studies show that it helps to decrease glucose and cholesterol levels and improve blood pressure. It is commonly used to treat breathing and digestion disorders. As it has anti-bacterial properties, it is used in herbal hand sanitisers. It is used as a base for making herbal mouth wash and toothpaste for treating bad breath, gum disease and mouth ulcers. Tulsi leaves can be washed and eaten raw. We can also add Tulsi leaves when we make tea. Tulsi tea helps in dealing with stress as it calms our mind. Tulsi protects us from a vast range of pollutants around us.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to the benefits of Tulsi (Holy Basil), a common herb. As you listen, complete the fact sheet given below.

question_bank.stem: Complete THE WONDER HERB fact sheet as you listen: (1) Name: Tulsi | Family: _______ (2) First found in: _______ (3) Now found: everywhere in India (4) Known as: The _______ of herbs (5) Can decrease levels of: _______ and cholesterol (6) Used to treat disorders of: breathing and _______ (7) Is used to make: mouth wash and _______ (8) We can have Tulsi leaves by: eating them _______ or adding them while making _______

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Daadi told Vikram and Vaibhavi about many spices. Choose one spice and introduce yourself as that spice to the class. Before you speak, make a spicy headband for the spice you chose (cut strips of paper 2 inches wide, join two ends to make a band, make a small card with a picture of the spice and colour it, attach the card on your headband). While speaking, mention: your name, part of the plant, colour, texture, taste, benefits.

Vikram's grandmother shared two natural cures to cure his cough in her earlier letter. Some of the words got washed away by the rain. Help Vikram complete the natural cures using the phrases from the box (two phrases not needed): some leaves of basil for cough, amount of honey, the mixture for high fever, a piece of fresh ginger, you can have it, grind to get, home remedy.

question_bank.stem: Complete natural cure 1 (ginger and honey cure for cough) by filling blanks with the correct phrases from the box.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, write a short paragraph on Tulsi based on the completed fact sheet in 'Let us listen'.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You have read the uses of spices in the letter. Complete the following table with the help of your teacher. Part of the plant: root/bark/leaves/flower buds/fruit/seeds. Texture: rough/soft/silky. Taste: bitter/sweet/sour/salty/spicy. Spices: Turmeric, Fenugreek, Cinnamon, Clove, Black pepper, Cardamom.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table for each spice — Part of plant, Colour, Texture, Taste. Spices: Turmeric, Fenugreek, Cinnamon, Clove, Black pepper, Cardamom.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the sentences from the letter (may, should, can, must, need to, used to are underlined). These are called modal verbs or modals. Each modal verb has a different function. (1) Match the helping verbs in Column A with their functions in Column B: (i) may — (ii) should — (iii) can — (iv) must — (v) need to — (vi) used to. Functions: A. past habit, B. suggestion, C. compulsion, D. advice, E. ability, F. necessity. (2) Complete the following dialogues with any four modals from the table above (use a modal only once).

question_bank.stem: Match modal verbs to their functions. Column A: (i) may, (ii) should, (iii) can, (iv) must, (v) need to, (vi) used to. Column B: A. past habit, B. suggestion, C. compulsion, D. advice, E. ability, F. necessity.

Complete the dialogue using any four modals (each used only once). Ajay: Anand had fever last week. He (i) _____________ take good rest. Suman: Yes, he (ii) _____________ or he will fall ill again. Ajay: He (iii) _____________ take grandmother's natural cures. Suman: Yes, we (iv) _____________ remember how effective they are.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now that you have read about many spices, can you guess the spices based on the riddles given below? (1) When your tummy felt funny / I was used by your granny / I made you fit and fine / And brought back your smile. Who am I? (2) I come from under the ground / Thousands of years, I've been around. / For cooking you use me daily / From cold I can cure you surely. Who am I?
